content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Honorable Robert C. Cheasty 
Mayor, City of Albany 
1000 San Pablo Avenue 
Albany, California 94706 
Dear Mayor Cheasty: 
December 17, 1987 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-87-294 
You have requested advice about the application of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act") to your actions on the board 
of directors of Albany News, Inc. 
QUESTION 
Does your position as Mayor of Albany limit your ability to 
participate in decisions of the board of directors of Albany 
News, Inc.? 
For example, may you participate in the board~s 
decision to charge the city of Albany the standard rate for 
publishing a public notice? 
CONCLUSION 
The Act does not limit your participation in the decisions 
of the newspaper's board of directors because its actions are 
not governmental decisions. 
Accordingly, you may participate 
in the newspaper's decision to charge the city of Albany the 
standard rate for publishing a public notice. 
Nevertheless, 
the Act may prohibit you from participating in or attempting to 
influence any decision before the city councilor other city 
officials if the decision would foreseeably and materially 
affect the newspaper. 
!/ Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California 
Administrative Code section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California 
Administrative Code. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • 
(916)322~5660 


Honorable Robert C. Cheasty 
December 17, 1987 
Page 2 
FACTS 
You are the mayor of the City of Albany. 
You also are on 
the board of directors of Albany News, Inc., a nonprofit 
Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c) (3) corporation, which 
publishes the Albany News. 
You are not paid for serving on the 
board. 
Of the five directors, four are elected or appointed 
public officials and the fifth has a record of public service. 
The directors are chosen because of their positions in the 
community so as to insure that the Albany News serves the 
public and to satisfy I.R.S. requirements for nonprofit 
status. 
The Albany News publishes public notices for the city of 
Albany. 
The newspaper wants to charge the city the standard 
rate for publishing public notices. 
The board will be 
considering approval of this charge. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act prohibits a public official from making, 
. 
participating in, or in any way attempting to use his official 
position to influence a governmental decision. 
(Section 87100.) 
You would be participating in a governmental decision if in 
your authority as mayor you voted on a matter, appointed a 
person, committed the city council to a course of action or 
entered into a contract on behalf of the council. 
(Regulation 
18700(b) through (d), copy enclosed.) 
Using your official 
position to influence a decision includes contacting city 
council members or other public employees or officials about a 
governmental decision. 
(Regulation 18700.1, copy enclosed.) 
Although you may have been chosen to serve on the board of 
directors of Albany News, Inc., because you are an elected 
official, in this case when you act as a board member you are 
not acting in your authority as mayor.~ The board's decisions 
are not governmental decisions. 
The Act does not apply to your 
duties on the board of directors. 
In fact, your duty of loyalty as a board member is to 
Albany News, Inc. and not the City of Albany. 
(See Pigeon 
~ Our present advice might differ if the city of Albany 
were a stockholder in Albany News, Inc., and the mayor were 
acting as a trustee for the city's stock holdings. 
(See 
Einboden Advice Letter, No. A-84-100, copy enclosed.) 


Honorable Robert C. Cheasty 
December 17, 1987 
Page 3 
Point Ranch, Inc. v. Perot (1963) 59 Cal.2d 227, 233; Cal. 
Corp. Code section 309(a).) 
For more information about your 
duties as a director, you may want to write to the state 
Department of corporations. 
On the other hand, if the Albany News were a source of 
income to you of $250 or more in the past calendar year and the 
Albany News filed a claim for payment that the Albany City 
council had to approve, you would be disqualified from voting 
on the claim. 
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18702.1(a) (1) and 
(b), copy enclosed.) 
voting on such a claim is a governmental 
decision to commit the city to pay the newspaper's claim for 
payment, and you would be acting within your authority as mayor 
and member of the city council. 
Moreover, you would be 
prohibited from communicating with city officials for the 
purpose of influencing the city's decision on that claim. 
(Regulation 18700.1, copy enclosed.) 
Of course, presently 
these restrictions do not apply because the Albany News is not 
a source of income to you of $250 or more in the past year. 
The provisions of Government Code Section 1090 also m~y 
apply to a decision about a contract between the city and the 
Albany News. 
The Commission does not administer this statute. 
We refer you to your city attorney for advice regarding section 
1090. 
I hope we have answered your request for advice. 
Please 
call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have a question about this 
letter.

Enclosures 
sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Robert C. Cheasty 
Mayor 
1000 San Pablo Avenue 
Albany, CA 
94706 
Dear Mayor Cheasty: 
November 24, 1987 
Re: 
87-294 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on November 23, 1987 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Margarita Altamirano, an 
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
Very truly yours, 
. . 
~~t;tt. ?jtA-ftitt/:) . 
Diane M. Griffiths 
/~Uy II(~ 
General Counsel

1000 SAN PABLO AVE .• ALBANY, CA 94706 • TELEPHONE 644_-8535

r? 
I 
OFFICE OF THE IMAVOR 
November 9, 1987 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 "J" Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, CA. 
95814 
Re: 
Potential Conflict of Interest 
To Whom It May Concern: 
I am requesting a written report in connection with my service on the Board 
of Directors of a non-profit Corporation, Albany News Inc •• 
Albany News Inc., publishes a newspaper on a monthly basis called Albany News. 
Albany News Inc. is a non-profit 501 (c)(3) corporation which has as its 
Board of Directors a number of public officials and people active in the 
community both to ensure the public service nature of the publication and to 
satisify the requirements of the I.R.S. in granting the 501 (c)(3) status. 
The City of Albany regularly publishes public notices in newspapers that 
reach general circulation in the City. 
The Albany News received one such 
public notice and published it. The Albany News would like to charge the 
City the standard rate for such publication. As I am on the City Council 
and also on the Board of Directors of Albany News Inc., the owner of the 
paper, I wanted to make sure there was no problem in applying the standard 
charge. 
From the newspaper's point of view, the other interests of a member 
of its Board of Directors should not stand in the way of the normal business 
of the newspaper. 
Additionally, one of the requirements of a non-profit corporation is that the 
the Board of Directors be "public" in some way and in considering whether to 
approve an application for a 501 (c)(3) the government will look to see whether 
members of the Board of Directors are public officials or in some way represent 
large public bodies. 
Toward that end, the Board of Directors of Albany News 
Inc. is comprised of five (5) people who are either elected or appointed 
officials with the exception of one (1) who has a record of public ~ervice and 
has a 
public profile in the City. 
Mayor

answer would be that I have no 
of 
a non-profit corporation which charges Albany 
publishing public notices.

of the 
paper, I wanted to make sure there was no problem in applying the standard

From the newspaper's point of view, the other interests of a member

of the newspaper. 
Additionally, one of the requirements of a non-profit corporation is that the 
the Board of Directors be "public" in some way and in considering whether to

members of the Board of Directors are public officials or in some way represent 
large public bodies. 
Toward that end, the Board of Directors of Albany News 
Inc. is comprised of five (5) people who are either elected or appointed 
with the exception of one (1) who has a record of public 
and 
in the City. 
The preferred answer would be that I have no conflict of
